CT HEAD W/O CONTRAST Study Date of [**2156-5-27**] 7:37 AM FINDINGS: There is no evidence of acute intracranial hemorrhage, edema, mass, mass effect, or large vascular territorial infarction.

EEG Study Date of [**2156-5-24**] FINDINGS:  SPIKE DETECTION PROGRAMS: No true interictal epileptiform discharges were seen;
; most of these were detections of somewhat sharply contoured background activity. A few sharp transients were seen, particularly in the bifrontal region,
these were not definitely epileptiform
SEIZURE DETECTION PROGRAMS: No true electrographic seizures were seen.

Most of these were detections of particularly rhythmic periods of a slow background. Selected review of the video demonstrated no apparent change in the patient's clinical status in association with these detections.
PUSHBUTTON ACTIVATIONS: There were none

CONTINUOUS EEG MONITORING: Review of time sampled excerpts of continuous EEG recording demonstrated that the background was typically of very low voltage and very slow usually in the delta frequency range although occasional intermixed theta frequency activity was seen at times. Bursts of generalized polymorphic delta frequency slowing and theta frequency slowing were also seen.
SLEEP: No normal sleep architecture was seen
CARDIAC MONITOR: Showed a generally regular rhythm.
This video EEG telemetry captured no pushbutton activations. No electrographic seizures or interictal epileptiform discharges were seen.

The background was mostly slow and disorganized throughout the day's recording suggestive of a moderate encephalopathy. Such an encephalopathy can be caused by hypoxic ischemic injury, medications, metabolic disturbances, and other etiologies.
EEG Study Date of [**2156-5-31**] FINDINGS: SPIKE DETECTION PROGRAMS: These were artifactual;
; no true interictal epileptiform discharges were seen.
SEIZURE DETECTION PROGRAMS: These were artifactual; no true electrographic seizures were seen.
no true electrographic seizures were seen.
PUSHBUTTON ACTIVATIONS: There were none.

ROUTINE TIME SAMPLING: Showed a background that did reach a normal alpha frequency at times during wakefulness but was more commonly slow and disorganized often in the theta and delta frequency ranges.
. At times, blunted sharp wave morphologies were seen over the occipital regions, particularly on the left, and sometimes recurred in the theta frequency range particularly over the O1 electrode.
Selected review of the video demonstrated no apparent change in the patient's appearance in association with these periods of sharp blunted theta activity.
SLEEP: No normal sleep morphologies were seen.
CARDIAC MONITOR: Showed a generally regular rhythm.
This video EEG telemetry captured no pushbutton activations. No electrographic seizures were seen.

There were some blunted sharp waves seen over the left occipital region sometimes recurrent in the theta frequency range for a few seconds at a time and these suggested a potential focus of epileptogenesis.
For most of the recording, the background was slightly slow and disorganized, suggestive of a mild encephalopathy.

Status post cardiac arrest:  Patient presented status post VF arrest
The etiology is unknown, but suspected to be related to electrolyte disturbances in the setting of alcohol abuse
positive cardiac enzymes which were likely elevated secondary to her cardiac arrest as opposed to primary ischemic event
ECG did not demonstrate t-wave inversions, st-elevations, or signs of ischemia, or prolonged QT interval

echocardiogram was done that showed normal LA, LV, RV cavity size, mild regional LV dysfunction w/ akinesis of the distal half of the anterior wall and severe hypokinesis of the remaining segments (LVEF = 25%).
Echo findings were thought to be secondary to post-cardiac arrest vs underlying cardiomyopathy.
underwent hypothermia protocol and then re-warming.
continued on amiodarone and pressors but these were stopped subsequently
was cooled using ArticSun according to post-arrest protocol

treated with therapeutic cooling

will be transferred to a neurological rehabilitation facility
